{{Short description|none}}
{{redirect|History of government|the academic field|political history}}
{{Politics sidebar|expanded=Primary}}
{{Human history}}
The political history of the [[world]] is the history of the various [[Politics|political entities]] created by the [[Human|human race]] throughout their existence and the way these states define their borders. Throughout [[Human history|history]], political systems have expanded from basic systems of [[self-governance]] and [[monarchy]] to the complex [[democracy|democratic]] and [[totalitarian]] systems that exist today. In parallel, political entities have expanded from vaguely defined frontier-type  [[Border|boundaries]], to the national definite boundaries existing today.

== Prehistoric era ==
{{Main|Prehistory}}
The [[Homo|primate ancestors of human beings]] already had social and political skills.<ref name="Fukuyama 2012">{{Cite book |last=Fukuyama |first=Francis |author-link=Francis Fukuyama |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eyEONn0WUmMC&pg=PA30 |title=The Origins of Political Order: From Prehuman Times to the French Revolution |date=2012 |publisher=Farrar, Straus and Giroux |isbn=978-0-374-53322-9 |language=en}}</ref>{{rp|p=30}} The first forms of human [[social organization]] were [[Family|families]] living in [[Band society|band societies]] as [[hunter-gatherer]]s.<ref name="Fukuyama 2012"/>{{rp|p=53}}

After the [[invention of agriculture]] around (7,000–8,000 BCE) across various parts of the world, human societies started transitioning to [[Tribe|tribal]] forms of organization.<ref name="Fukuyama 2012"/>{{rp|p=55}} Food surpluses made possible the development of a social elite who were not engaged in agriculture, industry or commerce, but dominated their communities and monopolized decision-making. Nonetheless, larger societies made it more feasible for people to adopt diverse decision making and governance models.<ref name="Eagly99">{{cite journal |last1=Eagly |first1=Alice H. |last2=Wood |first2=Wendy |date=June 1999 |title=The Origins of Sex Differences in Human Behavior: Evolved Dispositions Versus Social Roles |url=http://www.sscnet.ucla.edu/anthro/faculty/fiske/facets/eagly&wood.htm |url-status=dead |journal=American Psychologist |volume=54 |issue=6 |pages=408–423 |doi=10.1037/0003-066x.54.6.408 |bibcode=1999AmPsy..54..408E |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20000817071347/http://www.sscnet.ucla.edu/anthro/faculty/fiske/facets/eagly&wood.htm |archive-date=17 August 2000|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

There is evidence of diplomacy between different tribes, but also of [[endemic warfare]].<ref name="Holslag 2018">{{Cite book|last=Holslag|first=Jonathan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=6IZbDwAAQBAJ&q=a+political+history+of+the+world|title=A Political History of the World: Three Thousand Years of War and Peace|date=2018-10-25|publisher=Penguin UK|isbn=978-0-241-35205-2|language=en}}</ref>{{rp|p=26}} This could have been caused by theft of livestock or crops, [[Raptio|abduction of women]], or resource and status competition.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{page needed|date=January 2026}}

The [[three-age system]] of [[periodization]] of [[prehistory]] was first introduced for Scandinavia by [[Christian Jürgensen Thomsen]] in the 1830s. By the 1860s, it was embraced as a useful division of the "earliest history of mankind" in general<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |first1=Karl |last1=von Rotteck |first2=Karl Theodor |last2=Welcker |title=Menschheit |encyclopedia=Das Staats-Lexikon |language=de |trans-encyclopedia=The State Lexicon |year=1864 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kgL__ooMXj0C&pg=PA774 |page=774}}</ref> and began to be applied in [[Assyriology]]. The now-conventional periodization in the archaeology of the Ancient Near East was developed in the 1920s to 1930s.<ref>''Oriental Institute Communications'', Issues 13–19, Oriental Institute of the University of Chicago, 1922, p. 55.</ref>{{primary inline|reason=These issues would appear to be some of the works that developed this periodization i.e. a primary source for the claim about its role in doing that. Cannot possibly support "now-conventional". Need a secondary source that describes its role instead|date=January 2026}}

==Ancient history==
{{Main|Ancient history}}
The early distribution of political power was determined by the availability of [[fresh water]], [[Soil fertility|fertile soil]], and [[temperate climate]] of different locations.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=24–25}} These were all necessary for the development of highly organized societies.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pages=24–25}} The locations of these early societies were near, or benefiting from, the edges of [[Plate tectonics|tectonic plates]].<ref name="Dartnell 2019">{{Cite book|last=Dartnell|first=Lewis|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=g781DwAAQBAJ|title=Origins: How the Earth Shaped Human History|date=2019-01-31|publisher=Random House|isbn=978-1-4735-4733-9|language=en}}</ref>{{rp|p=25}}

The [[Indus Valley civilisation|Indus Valley Civilization]] was located next to the Himalayas (which were created by tectonic pressures) and the Indus and Ganges rivers, which deposit sediment from the mountains to produce fertile land.<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|pp=25–26}} A similar dynamic existed in [[Mesopotamia]], where the [[Tigris]] and [[Euphrates]] did the same with the [[Zagros Mountains]].<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|p=26}} [[Ancient Egypt]] was helped by the [[Nile]] depositing sediments from the East African highlands of its origins, while the [[Yellow River]] and [[Yangtze]] acted in the same way for Ancient China.<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|p=347}} Eurasia was advantaged in the development of agriculture by the natural occurrence of domesticable wild grass species and the east–west orientation of the landmass, allowing for the easy spread of domesticated crops.<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|p=87}} A similar advantage was given to it by half of the world's large mammal species living there, which could be domesticated.<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{page needed|date=January 2026}}

As the cooling and drying of the climate by 3800 BCE caused drought in Mesopotamia, village farmers began co-operating and started creating larger settlements with [[irrigation]] systems.<ref name="Fukuyama 2012"/>{{rp|p=70}} This wasnew water infrastructure in turn required centralised administration with complex social organisation.<ref name="Fukuyama 2012"/>{{rp|p=70}} However, there is archaeological evidence that shows similar successes with more egalitarian and decentralized complex societies.<ref>{{cite book |last=Roosevelt |first=Anna C. |title=Cambridge history of the Native peoples of the Americas: South America |volume=3 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |year=1999 |isbn=978-0-521-63075-7 |editor1-last=Salomon |editor1-first=Frank |pages=266–267 |chapter=The Maritime, Highland, Forest Dynamic and the Origins of Complex Culture |editor2-last=Schwartz |editor2-first=Stuart B. |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hxqgDcCrzjkC&pg=PA266 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160624045250/https://books.google.com/books?id=hxqgDcCrzjkC&pg=PA266 |archive-date=24 June 2016 |url-status=live}}</ref> The first cities and systems of greater social organisation emerged in Mesopotamia, followed within a few centuries by ones at the Indus and Yellow River Valleys.<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|p=73}} In the cities, the workforce could specialise as the whole population did not have to work for food production, while stored food allowed for large armies to create empires.<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|p=73}} The first [[empire]]s were those of [[Ancient Egypt]] and [[Mesopotamia]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=24–25}} Smaller kingdoms existed in [[North China Plain]], [[Indo-Gangetic Plain]], [[Central Asia]], [[Anatolia]], [[Eastern Mediterranean]], and [[Central America]], while the rest of humanity continued to live in small tribes.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=24–25}}

=== Middle East and the Mediterranean ===
{{See also|Cradle of civilization|Ancient Near East|Ancient Mediterranean}}
[[File:Ancient Orient.png|thumb|Overview map of the ancient Near East]]
The first states of sorts were those of [[Early Dynastic Period (Mesopotamia)|early dynastic Sumer]] and [[Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|early dynastic Egypt]], which arose from the [[Uruk period]] and [[Predynastic Egypt]] respectively at approximately 3000BCE.<ref name="Glyn 2003">{{cite book|last=Daniel|first=Glyn|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wx9FAAAAMAAJ|title=The First Civilizations: The Archaeology of their Origins|publisher=Phoenix Press|year=2003|isbn=1-84212-500-1|location=New York|pages=xiii|no-pp=true|author-link=Glyn Daniel|orig-year=1968}}</ref> Early dynastic [[Egypt]] was based around the [[Nile River]] in the north-east of [[Africa]], the kingdom's boundaries being based around the Nile and stretching to areas where [[oasis|oases]] existed.<ref name="Glyn 2003"/>{{rp|pp=9–11}} [[Upper Egypt|Upper]] and [[Lower Egypt]] were unified around 3150 BCE by Pharaoh [[Menes]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=33–34}} This process of consolidation was driven by the crowding of migrants from the expanding [[Sahara]] in the [[Nile Delta]].<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/> Nevertheless, political competition continued within the country between centers of power such as [[Memphis, Egypt|Memphis]] and [[Thebes, Egypt|Thebes]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=33–34}} The prevailing north-east trade winds made it easier to sail up the river, thereby helping the unification of the state.<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|p=72}} The geopolitical environment of the Egyptians had them surrounded by [[Nubia]] in the smaller southern oases of the Nile unreachable by boat, as well as by Libyan warlords operating from the oases around modern-day [[Benghazi]], and finally by raiders across the [[Sinai Peninsula|Sinai]] and the sea.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=34–35}} The country was well defended by natural barriers formed by the Sahara on both sides, though this also limited its ability to expand into a larger empire, mostly remaining a regional power along the Nile (except for a conquest of the Levant in the second millennium BCE).<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|p=72}} The lack of timber also made it too expensive to build a large navy for power projection across the Mediterranean or Red Seas.<ref name="Dartnell 2019"/>{{rp|p=73}}

==== Mesopotamian dominance ====
{{Main|History of Mesopotamia|Fertile Crescent}}
Mesopotamia is situated between the major rivers of [[Tigris]] and [[Euphrates]], and the first political power in the region was the [[Akkadian Empire]] starting around 2300 BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=39–40}} They were preceded by [[Sumer]], and later followed by [[Babylon]], and [[Assyria]]. They faced competition from the mountainous areas to the north, strategically positioned above the Mesopotamian plains, with kingdoms such as [[Mitanni]], [[Urartu]], [[Elam]], and [[Medes]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=39–40}} The Mesopotamians also innovated in governance by writing the first laws.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=39–40}}

A dry climate in the Iron Age caused turmoil as movements of people put pressure on the existing states resulting in the [[Late Bronze Age collapse]], with [[Cimmerians]], [[Arameans]], [[Dorians]], and the [[Sea Peoples]] migrating among others.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=41}} Babylon never recovered following the death of [[Hammurabi]] in 1699 BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=41}} Following this, [[Assyria]] grew in power under [[Adad-nirari II]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=58}} By the late ninth century BCE, the Assyrian Empire controlled almost all of Mesopotamia and much of the [[Levant]] and [[Anatolia]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=59}} Meanwhile, Egypt was weakened, eventually breaking apart after the death of [[Osorkon II]] until 710 BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=61}} In 853, the Assyrians fought and won a battle against a coalition of Babylon, Egypt, Persia, Israel, Aram, and ten other nations, with over 60,000 troops taking part according to contemporary sources.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=64}} However, the empire was weakened by internal struggles for power, and was plunged into a decade of turmoil beginning with a plague in 763 BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=64}} Following revolts by cities and lesser kingdoms against the empire, a [[coup d'état]] was staged in 745 by [[Tiglath-Pileser III]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=94}} He raised the army from 44,000 to 72,000, followed by his successor [[Sennacherib]] who raised it to 208,000, and finally by [[Ashurbanipal]] who raised an army of over 300,000.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=95}} This allowed the empire to spread over [[Cyprus]], the entire [[Levant]], [[Phrygia]], [[Urartu]], [[Cimmerians]], [[Iran|Persia]], [[Medes]], [[Elam]], and [[Babylon]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=95}}

==== Persian dominance ====
{{Main|Achaemenid Empire}}
By 650&nbsp;BCE, Assyria had started declining as a severe drought hit the Middle East and an alliance was formed against them.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=98}} Eventually they were replaced by the [[Median empire]] as the main power of the region following the [[Battle of Carchemish]] (605&nbsp;BCE) and the [[Battle of the Eclipse]] (585&nbsp;BCE).<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|rp=99}} The Medians served as the launching pad for the rise of the [[Persian Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=99}} After first serving as vassals, under the third Persian king [[Cambyses I]] their influence rose, and in 553&nbsp;BCE they rose against the Medians.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=99}} By the death of [[Cyrus the Great]], the Persian [[Achaemenid Empire]] reached from [[Aegean Sea]] to [[Indus River]] and [[Caucasus]] to [[Nubia]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=101}} The empire was divided into provinces ruled by [[satrap]]s, who collected taxes and were typically local power brokers.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=102}} The empire controlled about a third of the world's farm land and a quarter of its population.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=104}} In 522&nbsp;BCE, after King [[Cambyses II|Cambyses II's]] death, [[Darius the Great]] took over power.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=130}}

==== Greek dominance ====
{{Main|Ancient Greece}}
As the population of [[Ancient Greece]] grew, they began a [[Greek colonisation|colonization of the Mediterranean region]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=107}} This encouraged trade, which in turn caused political changes in the [[Polis|city-states]] with old elites being overthrown in [[Corinth]] in 657&nbsp;BCE and in [[Athens]] in 632, for example.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=108}} There were many wars between the cities as well, including the [[Messenian Wars]] (743–742&nbsp;BCE; 685–668&nbsp;BCE), the [[Lelantine War]] (710–650&nbsp;BCE), and the [[First Sacred War]] (595–585&nbsp;BCE).<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=108}} In the seventh and sixth centuries, [[Ancient Corinth|Corinth]] and [[Sparta]] were the dominant powers of Greece.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=109}} The former was eventually supplanted by [[Classical Athens|Athens]] as the main sea power, while Sparta remained the dominant land-force.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=109–110}} In 499&nbsp;BCE, in the [[Ionian Revolt]] Greek cities in [[Anatolia|Asia Minor]] rebelled against the Persian Empire but were crushed in the [[Battle of Lade]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=132}} After this, the Persians invaded the [[Geography of Greece|Greek mainland]] in the [[Greco-Persian Wars]] (499–449&nbsp;BCE).<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=132}}

The [[Macedonia (ancient kingdom)|Macedonian]] King [[Philip II of Macedon|Philip II]] (350–336&nbsp;BCE) conquered much of Greece.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{page needed|date=January 2026}} In 338&nbsp;BCE, he formed the [[League of Corinth]] to liberate Greeks in Asia Minor from the Persians, with 10,000 troops invading in 336&nbsp;BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{page needed|date=January 2026}} After his murder, his son [[Alexander the Great]] took charge and crossed the [[Dardanelles]] in 334&nbsp;BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=136}} After Asia Minor had been conquered, Alexander invaded Levant, Egypt, and Mesopotamia, defeating the Persians under [[Darius the Great]] in the [[Battle of Gaugamela]] in 331&nbsp;BCE, and ending the last resistance by 328&nbsp;BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=136}} After [[Death of Alexander the Great|Alexander's death]] in [[Babylon]] in 323&nbsp;BCE, the [[Wars of Alexander the Great|Macedonian Empire]] had no designated successor.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=137}} This led to its division into four: the [[Antigonid dynasty]] in [[Macedonia (ancient kingdom)|Macedonia]], the [[Attalid dynasty]] in [[Anatolia]], the [[Ptolemaic Kingdom]] in [[Ancient Egypt|Egypt]], and the [[Seleucid Empire]] over [[Mesopotamia]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=148}}

==== Roman dominance ====
{{Main|Ancient Rome}}
The [[Roman Republic]] became dominant in the [[Mediterranean Basin]] in the 3rd century BCE after defeating the [[Samnites]], the [[Gauls]] and the [[Etruscan civilization|Etruscans]] for control of the [[Italian Peninsula]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=149–150}} In 264&nbsp;BCE, it challenged its main rival [[Ancient Carthage|Carthage]] to a fight for [[Sicily]], starting the [[Punic Wars]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=188}} A truce was signed in 241&nbsp;BCE, with Rome gaining [[Corsica]] and [[Sardinia]] in addition to Sicily.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=188}} In 218&nbsp;BCE, the [[Military of Carthage|Carthaginian Army]] general [[Hannibal]] marched out of [[Carthaginian Iberia|Iberia]] towards Italy, [[Hannibal's crossing of the Alps|crossing the Alps]] with his [[war elephant]]s.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=189}} After 15 years of fighting, the [[Roman republican army]] beat him and then sent troops against Carthage itself, defeating it in 202&nbsp;BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=190}} The [[Second Punic War]] alone cost Rome 100,000 casualties.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=191}} In 146&nbsp;BCE, [[Siege of Carthage (Third Punic War)|Carthage was finally destroyed completely]] at the end of the [[Third Punic War]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=193}}

Rome suffered from various internal disturbances and instabilities. In 133&nbsp;BCE, [[Tiberius Gracchus]] was killed alongside hundreds of supporters after trying to redistribute [[public land]] to the poor under the [[Lex Sempronia Agraria]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=194}} The [[Social War (Italian)|Social War]] (91–88&nbsp;BCE) was caused by neighbouring cities trying to secure themselves the benefits of [[Roman citizenship]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=194}} In 82&nbsp;BCE, general [[Sulla]] captured power violently, ending the [[Roman Republic]] and becoming a [[Roman dictator|dictator]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=195}} Following his death new power struggles emerged, and in [[Caesar's Civil War]] (49–46&nbsp;BCE), [[Julius Caesar]] and [[Pompey]] fought over the empire, with the former winning.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=196}} After the [[assassination of Julius Caesar]] in 44&nbsp;BCE, a [[Year of the Four Emperors|second civil war]] broke out between his potential heirs, [[Mark Antony]] and [[Augustus]], the latter gaining the new title of [[Roman emperor]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=196}} This then led to the ''[[Pax Romana]]'', a long period of peace in the [[Roman Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=197}} The quarrels between the [[Ptolemaic Kingdom]], the [[Seleucid Empire]], the [[Parthian Empire]] and the [[Kingdom of Pontus]] in the Near East allowed the Romans to expand up to the [[Euphrates]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{page needed|date=January 2026}} During Augustus' reign the [[Rhine]], [[Danube]], and the [[Sahara]] became the other borders of the empire.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=210}} The population reached about 60 million.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=205}}

Political instability in Rome grew. Emperor [[Caligula]] (37–41&nbsp;CE) was murdered by the [[Praetorian Guard]] to replace him with [[Claudius]] (41–53), while his successor [[Nero]] (54–68) was rumored to have [[Great Fire of Rome|burned Rome down]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=213}} The average reign from his death to [[Philip the Arab]] (244–249) was six years.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=213}} Nevertheless, external expansion continued, with [[Trajan]] (98–117) invading [[Dacia]], [[Parthia]] and [[Arabian Peninsula|Arabia]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=214}} Its only formidable enemy was the [[Parthian Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=215}} Migrating peoples started exerting pressure on the borders of the empire in the [[Migration Period]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=218}} The drying climate of [[Central Asia]] forced the [[Huns]] to move, and in 370 they crossed [[Don (river)|Don]] and soon after the [[Danube]], forcing the [[Goths]] on the move, which in turn caused other [[Germanic peoples|Germanic tribes]] to overrun [[Borders of the Roman Empire|Roman borders]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=244–245}} In 293, [[Diocletian]] (284–305) appointed three rulers for different parts of the empire.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=249}} It was formally divided in 395 by [[Theodosius I]] (379–395) into the [[Western Roman Empire|Western Roman]] and [[Byzantine Empire]]s.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=252}} In 406 the northern border of the former was overrun by the [[Alemanni]], [[Vandals]] and [[Suebi]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=253}} In 408 the [[Visigoths]] invaded Italy and then [[Sack of Rome (410)|sacked Rome]] in 410.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=253}} The final collapse of the Western Empire came in 476 with the deposal of [[Romulus Augustulus]] (475–476).<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=254}}

=== Indian subcontinent ===
{{See also|Middle kingdoms of India}}

Built around the [[Indus River]], by 3300 BCE the [[Indus Valley Civilization]], located in modern-day India, Pakistan and Afghanistan, had formed. The civilization's boundaries extended to 600&nbsp;km from the [[Arabian Sea]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Daniels |first=Patrica S |author2=Stephen G Hyslop |author3=[[Douglas Brinkley]] |author4=Esther Ferington |author5=Lee Hassig |author6=Dale-Marie Herring |editor=Toni Eugene |title=Almanac of World History |year=2003 |publisher=[[National Geographic Society]] |isbn=0-7922-5092-3 |pages=56 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=d5gPAQAACAAJ }}{{Dead link|date=May 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> After its cities [[Mohenjo-daro]] and [[Harappa]] were abandoned around 1900 BCE, no political power is known to have replaced it.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{page needed|date=January 2026}}

States began to form in 12th century BCE with the formation of [[Kuru kingdom]] which was first state level administration in Indian subcontinent. In 6th century BCE with the emergence of [[Mahajanapadas]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=82}} Out of sixteen such states, four strong ones emerged: [[Kosala]], [[Magadha (Mahajanapada)|Magadha]], [[Vatsa]], and [[Avanti (Ancient India)|Avanti]], with Magadha dominating the rest by the mid-fifth century.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=115}} The Magadha then transformed into the [[Nanda Empire]] under [[Mahapadma Nanda]] (345–321&nbsp;BCE), extending from the [[Indo-Gangetic Plain|Gangetic plains]] to the [[Hindu Kush]] and the [[Deccan Plateau]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=153}} The empire was, however, overtaken by [[Chandragupta Maurya]] (324–298&nbsp;BCE), turning it into the [[Maurya Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=153}} He defended against Alexander's invasion from the West and received control of the Hindu Kush mountain passes in a peace treaty signed in 303&nbsp;BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=153}} By the time of his grandson [[Ashoka]]'s rule, the empire stretched from [[Zagros Mountains]] to the [[Brahmaputra River]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=154}} The empire contained a population of 50 to 60 million, governed by a system of provinces ruled by governor-princes, with a capital in [[Pataliputra]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=155}}

After Ashoka's death, the empire had begun to decline, with [[Kashmir]] in the north, [[Shunga Empire|Shunga]] and [[Satavahana dynasty|Satavahana]] in the centre, and [[Kalinga (historical region)|Kalinga]] as well as [[Pandya dynasty|Pandya]] in the south becoming independent.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=183}} In to this [[power vacuum]], the [[Yuezhi]] were able to establish the new [[Kushan Empire]] in 30 CE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=185}}  The [[Gupta Empire]] was founded by [[Chandragupta I]] (320–335), which in sixty years expanded from the [[Ganges]] to the [[Bay of Bengal]] and the [[Indus River]] following the downfall of the Kushan Empire.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=268}} Gupta governance was similar to that of the Maurya.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=269}} Following wars with the [[Hephthalites]] and other problems, the empire fell by 550.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=271}}  
[[File:Qin empire 210 BCE.jpg|thumb|Qin dynasty]]

=== China ===
{{See also|Ancient China}}
In the [[North China Plain]], the [[Yellow River]] allowed the rise of states such as [[Wei (state)|Wei]] and [[Qi (state)|Qi]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=42–43}} This area was first unified by the [[Shang dynasty]] around 1600 BCE, and replaced by the [[Zhou dynasty]] in the [[Battle of Muye]] in 1046 BCE, with reportedly millions taking part in the fighting.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|pp=4–439}} The victors were however hit by internal unrest soon after.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=76}} The main rivals of the Zhou were the [[Dongyi]] in [[Shandong]], the [[Xianyun]] in [[Ordos Desert|Ordos]], the [[Guifang]] in [[Shanxi]], as well as the [[Chu (state)|Chu]] in the middle reaches of the [[Yangtze]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=78}}

Beginning in the eighth century BCE China, fell into chaos for five centuries during the [[Spring and Autumn period|Spring and Autumn]] (771–476&nbsp;BCE) and [[Warring States period]]s (476–221&nbsp;BCE).<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=81}} During the latter period,{{clarify inline|reason=The Jin were 400 years after the period discussed here!|date=January 2026}} the [[Jin dynasty (266–420)|Jin dynasty]] split into the [[Wei (state)|Wei]], [[Zhao (state)|Zhao]] and [[Han (Warring States)|Han]] states, while the rest of the North China Plain was composed of the [[Chu (state)|Chu]], [[Qin (state)|Qin]], [[Qi (state)|Qi]] and [[Yan (state)|Yan]] states, while the Zhou remained in the centre with largely ceremonial power.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=157}} While the Zhao had an advantage at first, the Qin ended up defeating them in 260&nbsp;BCE with about half a million soldiers fighting on each side at the [[Battle of Changping]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=157–158}} The other states tried to form an alliance against the Qin but were defeated.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=171}} In 221&nbsp;BCE, the [[Qin dynasty]] was established with a population of about 40 million, with a capital of 350,000 in [[Ancient Linzi|Linzi]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=158}} Under the leadership of [[Qin Shi Huang]], the dynasty initiated reforms such as establishing territorial administrative units, infrastructure projects (including the [[Great Wall of China]]) and uniform [[Chinese characters]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=172}} However, after his death and burial with the [[Terracotta Army]], the empire started falling apart when the Chu and Han started fighting over a power vacuum left by a weak heir, with the [[Han dynasty]] rising to power in 204&nbsp;BCE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=173}}

Under the Han, the population of China rose to 50 million, with 400,000 in the capital [[Chang'an]], and with territorial expansion to [[Korea]], [[Vietnam]] and [[Tian Shan|Tien Shan]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=175}} Expeditions were also sent against the [[Xiongnu]] and to secure the [[Hexi Corridor]], the [[Nanyue]] kingdom was annexed, and [[Hainan]] and [[Taiwan]] conquered.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=180}} The Chinese pressure on the Xiongnu forced them towards the west, leading to the exodus of the [[Yuezhi]], who in turn pillaged the capital of [[Bactria]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=182}} This then led to their new [[Kushan Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=185}} The end of the Han dynasty in 220&nbsp;CE came following internal upheavals, with its split into the [[Shu Han|Shu]], [[Eastern Wu|Wu]] and [[Cao Wei|Wei]] states.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/> Following a brief unification under the [[Jin dynasty (266–420)]], China was divided again in 304 due to the [[Invasion and rebellion of the Five Barbarians|rebellion of the Five Barbarians]] (304–316). Northern China and Sichuan were ruled by the [[Sixteen Kingdoms]], while the Jin relocated south of the [[Yangtze|Yangtze River]]. By 439, the [[Xianbei]]-led [[Northern Wei]] unified the north while the Jin was usurped by the [[Liu Song dynasty|Liu Song]], transitioning into the [[Northern and Southern dynasties]] period. China would be unified by the [[Sui dynasty]] in 589 CE.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=260}}

=== Americas ===
{{See also|Pre-Columbian era}}
The [[Olmecs]] were the first major [[Indigenous peoples of the Americas|Indigenous American]] culture, with some smaller ones such as the [[Chavín culture]] amongst mainly hunter-gatherers.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=83}} The Olmecs were limited by the dense forests and the long [[Wet season|rainy season]] of the [[Olmec heartland]], as well as the lack of horses.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=84}}

== Post-classical era ==
{{main|Post-classical history}}

=== Africa ===
The coast of [[East Africa]] contained a string of trading cities connected to kingdoms in the interior.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=423}} The [[Horn of Africa]] was dominated by the [[Ethiopian Empire]] by the 13th and 14th centuries.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=423}} South from it were the [[Swahili people|Swahili]] cities of [[Mogadishu]], [[Mombasa]], [[Zanzibar]], [[Kilwa Sultanate|Kilwa]], and [[Sofala]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=424}} By the 14th century, Kilwa had conquered most of the others.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=424}} It also engaged in campaigns against the inland power of [[Great Zimbabwe]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=424}} Great Zimbabwe was itself overtaken in trade by its rival, the [[Kingdom of Mutapa]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=424}} Towards the north, the Empire of Kitara dominated the [[African Great Lakes]] in the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=425}} Towards the Atlantic coast, the [[Kingdom of Kongo]] was of regional importance around the same time.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=425}} The [[Gulf of Guinea]] had the [[Kingdom of Benin]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=425}} To the north, in the [[Sahel]], there was a tripartite competition between the [[Mossi Kingdoms]], the [[Songhai Empire]], as well as the [[Mali Empire]], with the latter declining in the fifteenth century.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=426}}

=== Americas ===
The [[Tiwanaku Empire|Tiwanaku Polity]] in western [[Bolivia]] was based in the southern [[Lake Titicaca]] Basin. Its influence extended into present-day [[Peru]] and [[Chile]] and lasted from around 600 to 1000 AD.<ref name="Marsh2019">{{cite journal|last=Marsh|first=Erik|date=2019|title=Temporal Inflection Points in Decorated Pottery: A Bayesian Refinement of the Late Formative Chronology in the Southern Lake Titicaca Basin, Bolivia|journal=Latin American Antiquity|volume=30|issue=4|pages=798–817|doi=10.1017/laq.2019.73|doi-access=free|s2cid=213080578|hdl=11336/175804|hdl-access=free}}</ref> [[Chimor]] was the political grouping of the [[Chimú culture]] that ruled the northern coast of Peru beginning around 850 and ending around 1470. Chimor was the largest kingdom in the [[Cultural periods of Peru|Late Intermediate period]], encompassing {{convert|1000|km}} of coastline. The [[Aymara kingdoms]] in turn were a group of native polities that flourished towards the [[Late Intermediate Period]], after the fall of the Tiwanaku Empire, whose societies were geographically located in the [[Qullaw]]. They were developed between 1150 and 1477, before the kingdoms disappeared due to the military conquest of the [[Inca Empire]].

Beginning around 250 AD, the [[Maya civilization]] developed many [[city-state]]s linked by a complex [[Trade in Maya civilization|trade network]]. In the [[Yucatán Peninsula|Maya Lowlands]] two great rivals, the cities of [[Tikal]] and [[Calakmul]], became powerful. The period also saw the intrusive intervention of the central Mexican city of [[Teotihuacan]] in Maya dynastic politics. In the 9th century, there was a widespread [[Classic Maya collapse|political collapse]] in the central Maya region, resulting in [[internecine war]]fare, the abandonment of cities, and a northward shift of population. The Postclassic period saw the rise of [[Chichen Itza]] in the north, and the expansion of the aggressive [[Kʼicheʼ kingdom of Qʼumarkaj|Kʼicheʼ kingdom]] in the Guatemalan Highlands. In the 16th century, the [[Spanish Empire]] colonised the Mesoamerican region, and a lengthy series of campaigns saw the fall of [[Nojpetén]], the last Maya city, in 1697.

The [[Aztec Empire]] was formed as an alliance of three [[Nahua peoples|Nahua]] {{lang|nci|[[altepetl]]}} city-states: {{lang|nci|[[Tenochtitlan|Mexico-Tenochtitlan]]|italic=no}}, {{lang|nci|[[Tetzcoco (altepetl)|Tetzcoco]]|italic=no}}, and {{lang|nci|[[Tlacopan]]|italic=no}}.  from the victorious factions of a civil war fought between the city of {{lang|nci|[[Azcapotzalco (altepetl)|Azcapotzalco]]|italic=no}} and its former tributary provinces. These three city-states ruled the area in and around the [[Valley of Mexico]] from 1428 until the combined forces of the Spanish {{lang|es|[[conquistador]]es}} and their native allies under {{lang|es|[[Hernán Cortés]]|italic=no}} defeated them in 1521. Despite the initial conception of the empire as an alliance of three self-governed city-states, {{lang|nci|[[Tenochtitlan]]|italic=no}} quickly became dominant militarily.<ref name="Hassig 1988">Hassig 1988</ref> By the time the Spanish arrived in 1519, the lands of the Alliance were effectively ruled from {{lang|nci|Tenochtitlan|italic=no}}, while the other partners in the alliance had taken subsidiary roles. The [[Tarascan state]] was the second-largest state in [[Mesoamerica]] at the time.<ref>{{cite web|first=Julie |last=Adkins |title=Mesoamerican Anomaly? The Pre-Conquest Tarascan State|url=http://faculty.smu.edu/rkemper/anth_3311/anth_3311_adkins_tarascan_paper.htm|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091219132626/http://faculty.smu.edu/rkemper/anth_3311/anth_3311_adkins_tarascan_paper.htm|archive-date=19 December 2009|access-date=19 April 2018|website=smu.edu}}</ref> It was founded in the early 14th century.

=== Asia ===
[[File:Tang Dynasty circa 700 CE.png|thumb|left|The [[Tang dynasty]] in 700 CE]]
When [[China]] entered the [[Sui dynasty]],<ref>{{cite book |last=Benn |first=Charles D. |title=China's Golden Age: Everyday Life in the Tang Dynasty |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ile3jSveb4sC |access-date=2008-12-17 |year=2004 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=0-19-517665-0 |pages=1}}</ref> the government changed and expanded in its borders as the many separate bureaucracies unified under one banner.<ref>{{cite book |last=Daniels |first=Patrica S |author2=Stephen G Hyslop |author3=Douglas Brinkley |author4=Esther Ferington |author5=Lee Hassig |author6=Dale-Marie Herring |editor=Toni Eugene |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=d5gPAQAACAAJ |title=Almanac of World History |year=2003 |publisher=National Geographic Society |isbn=0-7922-5092-3 |pages=118–21 }}{{Dead link|date=May 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> This evolved into the [[Tang dynasty]] when [[Emperor Gaozu of Tang|Li Yuan]] took control of China in 626.<ref>{{cite book |last=Benn |first=Charles D. |title=China's Golden Age: Everyday Life in the Tang Dynasty |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ile3jSveb4sC |access-date=2008-12-17 |year=2004 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=0-19-517665-0 |pages=ix}}</ref> By now, the [[Borders of China|Chinese borders]] had expanded from [[East China|eastern China]], up north into the Tang Empire.<ref name="chinaatlas">{{cite book |last=Herrmann |first=Albert |title=Historical and Commercial Atlas of China |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=5YnMGwAACAAJ |access-date=2008-12-17 |year=1970 |publisher=Ch'eng-wen Publishing House}}</ref> The Tang Empire fell apart in 907 and split into [[Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period|ten regional kingdoms and five dynasties]] with vague borders.<ref>{{cite book |last=Hucker |first=Charles O. |title=China's Imperial Past: An Introduction to Chinese History and Culture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wdqoHQRUhAYC |access-date=2008-12-17 |year=1995 |publisher=Stanford University Press |isbn=0-8047-2353-2 |pages=147}}</ref> Fifty-three years after the separation of the Tang Empire, China entered the [[Song dynasty]] under the rule of [[Chao K'uang-yin|Chao K'uang]], although the [[border]]s of this country expanded, they were never as large as those of the Tang dynasty and were constantly being redefined due to attacks from the neighboring [[Tatars|Tartar]] (Mongol) people known as the [[Khitan people|Khitan tribes]].<ref name="almanac">{{cite book |last=Daniels |first=Patrica S |author2=Stephen G Hyslop |author3=Douglas Brinkley |author4=Esther Ferington |author5=Lee Hassig |author6=Dale-Marie Herring |editor=Toni Eugene |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=d5gPAQAACAAJ |title=Almanac of World History |year=2003 |publisher=National Geographic Society |isbn=0-7922-5092-3 |pages=134–5 }}{{Dead link|date=May 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>

The [[Mongol Empire]] emerged from the unification of several [[Nomad|nomadic tribes]] in the [[Mongol]] homeland under the [[leadership]] of [[Genghis Khan]] ({{circa|1162}}–1227), whom a council proclaimed as the ruler of all Mongols in 1206. The empire grew rapidly under his rule and that of his descendants, who sent out [[Mongol invasions|invading armies]] in every direction.<ref>Diamond. ''Guns, Germs, and Steel''. p. 367.</ref><ref>''The Mongols and Russia'', by [[George Vernadsky]]</ref> The vast transcontinental empire connected the [[Eastern world|East]] with the [[Western world|West]], the [[Pacific Ocean|Pacific]] to the [[Mediterranean Sea|Mediterranean]], in an enforced ''[[Pax Mongolica]]'', allowing the dissemination and exchange of trade, technologies, commodities and ideologies across [[Eurasia]].<ref>Gregory G.Guzman "Were the barbarians a negative or positive factor in ancient and medieval history?", ''The Historian'' 50 (1988), 568–70.</ref><ref>Allsen. ''Culture and Conquest''. p. 211.</ref> The Mongol invasion halted China's economic development for over 150 years, decisively changing the balance of power in the [[Eastern Hemisphere]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=361}}

The empire began to split due to wars over succession, as the grandchildren of Genghis Khan disputed whether the royal line should follow from his son and initial heir [[Ögedei Khan|Ögedei]] or from one of his other sons, such as [[Tolui]], [[Chagatai Khan|Chagatai]], or [[Jochi]]. The Toluids prevailed after a bloody purge of Ögedeid and Chagataid factions, but disputes continued among the descendants of Tolui. After [[Möngke Khan]] died (1259), rival [[kurultai]] councils simultaneously elected different successors, the brothers [[Ariq Böke]] and [[Kublai Khan]], who fought each other in the [[Toluid Civil War]] (1260–1264) and also dealt with challenges from the descendants of other sons of Genghis.<ref>{{cite web|year=1998|title=The Islamic World to 1600: The Golden Horde|url=https://www.ucalgary.ca/applied_history/tutor/islam/mongols/goldenHorde.html|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101113102742/http://www.ucalgary.ca/applied_history/tutor/islam/mongols/goldenHorde.html|archive-date=13 November 2010|access-date=3 December 2010|publisher=University of Calgary}}</ref><ref>Michael Biran. ''Qaidu and the Rise of the Independent Mongol State in Central Asia''. The Curzon Press, 1997, {{ISBN|0-7007-0631-3}}</ref> Kublai successfully took power, but civil war ensued as he sought unsuccessfully to regain control of the [[List of Chagatai Khans|Chagatayid]] and [[House of Ögedei|Ögedeid]] families. By the time of Kublai's death in 1294 the Mongol Empire had [[Division of the Mongol Empire|fractured into four separate khanates or empires]], each pursuing its own separate interests and objectives: the [[Golden Horde]] khanate in the northwest, the [[Chagatai Khanate]] in Central Asia, the [[Ilkhanate]] in the southwest, and the [[Yuan dynasty]] in the east, based in modern-day [[Beijing]].<ref name="China p413">''The Cambridge History of China: Alien Regimes and Border States''. p. 413.</ref>

In 1304, the three western khanates briefly accepted the nominal [[suzerainty]] of the Yuan dynasty,<ref>Jackson. ''Mongols and the West''. p. 127.</ref><ref>Allsen. ''Culture and Conquest''. pp. xiii, 235.</ref> but in 1368 the [[Han Chinese]] [[Ming dynasty]] took over the Mongol capital. The Genghisid rulers of the Yuan retreated to the Mongolian homeland and continued to rule there as the [[Northern Yuan dynasty]]. The Ming dynasty, the largest army in the world, with almost a million soldiers.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=419}} It was therefore able to conduct military campaigns in [[Manchuria]], [[Inner Mongolia]], [[Yunnan]], and [[Vietnam]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=419}} Naval voyages were also sent, with the [[Ming treasure voyages]] reaching [[Africa]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=419}} These also intervened militarily in [[Java]], [[Sumatra]], and [[Sri Lanka]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=420}} The Ilkhanate disintegrated in the period 1335–1353. The Golden Horde had broken into competing khanates by the end of the 15th century and was defeated and thrown out of Russia in 1480 by the [[Grand Duchy of Moscow]] while the Chagatai Khanate lasted in one form or another until 1687.

=== Middle East and Europe ===
{{Main|Islamic Golden Age|Middle Ages}}
The [[Byzantine–Sasanian War of 572–591|Byzantine–Sasanian Wars of 572–591]] and [[Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628|602–628]] produced the cumulative effects of a century of almost continuous conflict, leaving both empires crippled. When [[Kavad II|Kavadh II]] died only months after coming to the throne, the [[Sasanian Empire]] was plunged into several years of dynastic turmoil and civil war. The Sasanians were further weakened by economic decline, heavy taxation from [[Khosrow II|Khosrau II]]'s campaigns, religious unrest, and the increasing power of the [[Dehqan|provincial landholders]].<ref>Howard-Johnston (2006), 9: "[Heraclius'] victories in the field over the following years and its political repercussions&nbsp;... saved the main bastion of Christianity in the Near East and gravely weakened its old Zoroastrian rival."</ref> The [[Byzantine Empire]] was also severely affected, with its financial reserves exhausted by the war and the [[Balkans]] now largely in the hands of the [[Slavs]].<ref>Haldon (1997), 43–45, 66, 71, 114–15</ref> Additionally, Anatolia was devastated by repeated Persian invasions; the Empire's hold on its recently regained territories in the Caucasus, Syria, Mesopotamia, Palestine and Egypt was loosened by many years of Persian occupation.<ref>Ambivalence toward Byzantine rule on the part of [[Miaphysitism|miaphysites]] may have lessened local resistance to the Arab expansion (Haldon [1997], 49–50).</ref> Neither empire was given any chance to recover, and according to George Liska, the "unnecessarily prolonged Byzantine–Persian conflict opened the way for Islam".<ref>Liska (1998), 170</ref>

The [[Quraysh]] ruled the city of [[Mecca]], and expelled their member [[Muhammad]] from it to the city of Medina in 622, from where he began spreading his new religion, [[Islam]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=286}} In 631 Muhammad marched with 10,000 to Mecca and conquered it before dying the next year.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=286}} His successors united most of Arabia in the [[Ridda wars]] (632–633) and then started the [[Muslim conquest of the Levant|Muslim conquests of the Levant]] (634–641), [[Muslim conquest of Egypt|Egypt]] (639–642) and [[Muslim conquest of Persia|Persia]] (633–651), the latter ending the Sasanian empire.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=286}} In less than a decade after his death, the Islamic [[Rashidun Caliphate]] extended its reach from [[Atlas Mountains]] in the west to the [[Hindu Kush]] in the east.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=287}} However, the [[First Fitna]] led to its replacement by the [[Umayyad Caliphate]] in 661, moving the centre of power to [[Damascus]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=286}} At its height, the Umayaads ruled a third of the world's population.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=288}} In 750, the [[Abbasid Caliphate]] replaced the Umayyads in the [[Abbasid Revolution]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=338}} In 762, they moved the capital to [[Baghdad]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=341}} The [[Emirate of Córdoba]] remained under Umayaad rule, while in 788 the [[Idrisid dynasty]] broke away in [[Morocco]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=343}} The [[Fatimid Caliphate]] started taking over North Africa from 909 onwards, and the [[Buyid dynasty]] broke away in Persia and later Mesopotamia starting in the 930s.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=286}}
In 711, the [[Umayyad conquest of Hispania]] began, and in 717 they crossed the [[Pyrenees]] into the [[European Plain]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=297}} They were met by the [[Merovingian dynasty]], which had been established by [[Clovis I]] (481–511), which was in decline, leading [[Charles Martel]] to seize power and defeat the invasion force at the [[Battle of Tours]] in 732.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=297}} His son [[Pepin the Short]] established the [[Carolingian dynasty]] in 751.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=297}} [[Charlemagne]] (768–814) turned it into the [[Carolingian Empire]], being crowned Emperor of the Romans in 800 by the Pope, with this forming the basis for the later [[Holy Roman Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=344}} Meanwhile, in Eastern Europe, [[Krum]] (795–814) expanded the [[First Bulgarian Empire|Bulgarian Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=345}} The [[Treaty of Verdun]] divided Carolingian Empire into [[West Francia|West]], [[Middle Francia|Middle]] and [[East Francia]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=348}}

During the [[Viking Age]] (793–1066 AD), [[Norsemen]] known as [[Vikings]] undertook large-scale raiding, colonizing, conquest, and trading throughout Europe, and reached North America.<ref name="Mawer">{{cite book|last=Mawer|first=Allen|url=https://archive.org/details/vikings00mawe|title=The Vikings|publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]]|year=1913|isbn=095173394X|page=[https://archive.org/details/vikings00mawe/page/1 1]|quote=The term 'Viking' is derived from the Old Norse ''vík'', a bay, and means 'one who haunts a bay, creek or fjord'. In the 9th and 10th centuries it came to be used more especially of those warriors who left their homes in Scandinavia and made raids on the chief European countries. This is the narrow, and technically the only correct use of the term 'Viking,' but in such expressions as 'Viking civilisation,' 'the Viking Age,' 'the Viking movement,' 'Viking influence,' the word has come to have a wider significance and is used as a concise and convenient term for describing the whole of the civilisation, activity and influence of the Scandinavian peoples, at a particular period in their history...|author-link=Allen Mawer}}</ref><ref name="Sawyer_1995">{{cite book|last=Sawyer|first=Peter H.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=owygAAAAMAAJ|title=Scandinavians and the English in the Viking Age|publisher=[[University of Cambridge]]|year=1995|isbn=095173394X|page=3|quote=The Viking period is, therefore, best defined as the period when Scandinavians played a large role in the British Isles and western Europe as raiders and conquerors. It is also the period in which Scandinavians settled in many of the areas they conquered, and in the Atlantic islands...|author-link=Peter Sawyer (historian)}}</ref> Voyaging by sea from their homelands in [[Denmark]], [[Norway]] and [[Sweden]], the Norse people settled in the [[Viking activity in the British Isles|British Isles]], [[History of Ireland (800–1169)|Ireland]], the [[Faroe Islands]], [[Settlement of Iceland|Iceland]], [[History of Greenland#Norse settlement|Greenland]], [[History of Normandy|Normandy]], the [[Baltic Sea|Baltic coast]], and along the [[Trade route from the Varangians to the Greeks|Dnieper]] and [[Volga trade route]]s in eastern Europe, where they were also known as [[Varangians]]. They also briefly [[Norse colonization of North America|settled in Newfoundland]], becoming the first Europeans to reach North America. The Vikings founded several kingdoms and earldoms in Europe: the [[kingdom of the Isles]] (''Suðreyjar''), [[Earldom of Orkney|Orkney]] (''Norðreyjar''), [[Scandinavian York|York]] (''Jórvík'') and the [[Danelaw]] (''Danalǫg''), [[Kingdom of Dublin|Dublin]] (''Dyflin''), [[Duchy of Normandy|Normandy]], and [[Kievan Rus']] (''[[Garðaríki]]''). The Norse homelands were also unified into larger kingdoms during the Viking Age, and the short-lived [[North Sea Empire]] included large swathes of Scandinavia and Britain.

In 1095, [[Pope Urban II]] proclaimed the [[First Crusade]] at the [[Council of Clermont]]. He encouraged military support for [[Byzantine Emperor]] [[Alexios I Komnenos|Alexios{{nbsp}}I]] against the [[Seljuk Empire|Seljuk Turks]] and an armed pilgrimage to Jerusalem. Across all social strata in western Europe there was an enthusiastic popular response. Volunteers took a public vow to join the crusade. Historians now debate the combination of their motivations, which included the prospect of mass ascension into Heaven at Jerusalem, satisfying feudal obligations, opportunities for renown, and economic and political advantage. Initial successes established four [[Crusader states]] in the [[Near East]]: the [[County of Edessa]]; the [[Principality of Antioch]]; the [[Kingdom of Jerusalem]]; and the [[County of Tripoli]]. The crusader presence remained in the region in some form until the city of [[Siege of Acre (1291)|Acre fell]] in 1291, leading to the rapid loss of all remaining territory in the [[Levant]]. After this, there were no further [[crusades]] to recover the Holy Land.

Following the end of the [[Carolingian Empire]], the largest polities in Western Europe were the [[Holy Roman Empire]], the [[Byzantine Empire]], [[Kingdom of France]], and the [[Kingdom of England]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=370}} The [[Catholic Church]] also wielded tremendous power.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=370}} In Eastern Europe, the [[Mongol invasion of Europe]] killed half the population 1237 to 1241.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=369}} The resulting power vacuum helped the [[Teutonic Order]], while the [[Kingdom of Poland (1025–1385)|Kingdom of Poland]] and the [[Kingdom of Hungary]] became the main Catholic realms.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=375}} Further east, the [[Kievan Rus']] continued to prosper.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=375}} The main power to the south meanwhile was the [[Byzantine Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=375}} However, by 1180, the [[Republic of Venice]] had changed the balance of maritime power in the Mediterranean.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=377}} In the [[Greater Middle East]], power was divided between the [[Seljuk Empire]], the [[Fatimid Caliphate]], the [[Buyid dynasty]], and the [[Ghaznavids]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=378}} No Islamic power was able to hold Egypt, the Levant, Mesopotamia, and Persia at the same time again.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=382}} In 1258, the Mongol [[Siege of Baghdad (1258)|Siege of Baghdad]] pushed the Islamic world into disarray.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=381}}

The [[Seljuk dynasty]] was founded by [[Osman I]] (1200–1323), leading to the [[Ottoman Empire]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=408}} In 1345, the Ottomans entered Europe across the [[Dardanelles]], conquering [[Thessaloniki]] in 1387, and advancing to [[Kosovo]] by 1389.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=409}} The [[Fall of Constantinople]] followed in 1453.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=409}} The Fall of Constantinople marked the end of the Byzantine Empire, and effectively the end of the [[Roman Empire]], a state which dated back to 27 BC and lasted nearly 1,500 years. The conquest of Constantinople and the fall of the Byzantine Empire was a key event of the [[Late Middle Ages]] and is considered the end of the [[Middle Ages|Medieval period]].

=== Indian subcontinent ===

Indian politics revolved around the struggle between the Buddhist [[Pala Empire]], the Hindu [[Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty]], the Jainist [[Rashtrakuta dynasty]], as well as the Islamic caliphate.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=333}} The Pala Empire had risen around 750 in [[Bengal]] under [[Gopala I]], while the Rashtrakutas had emerged around the same time in the [[Deccan Plateau]] and the southern coast under [[Dantidurga]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=334}} The Pratiharas first united the [[Indo-Gangetic Plain]] under [[Nagabhata I]] (c. 730–760), who has defeated an Islamic invasion of northern India.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=334}} The struggle between the four lasted for almost 200 years.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=335}} By the ninth century, the [[Ghaznavids]], a breakaway from the caliphate, arose after taking advantage of the others' internal weaknesses.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=335}}

The [[Chola dynasty]] arose as the one of Asia's strongest trading powers before invading [[Sri Lanka]] at the end of the 900's.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=383}} In 1025, they attacked rival commercial kingdom of [[Srivijaya]] in Southeast Asia.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=383}} Their enemies in India included an alliance of [[Pandya dynasty|Pandyan]] princes and the [[Chalukya dynasty]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=383}} However, the [[Ghurid dynasty]] invaded the northern parts of the subcontinent 1175 to 1186, conquering much of them.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=385}}<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=412}} In 1206, [[Qutb al-Din Aibak]] founded the [[Delhi Sultanate]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=412}} By the 14th century, it controlled the [[Indo-Gangetic Plain]] and the [[Deccan Plateau]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=412}} In the middle of the century, the latter saw the rise of the [[Vijayanagara Empire]], which ruled much of southern India as a federation.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=413}} The Sultanate and the Empire engaged in continuous warfare without either being able to defeat the other.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=413}}

== Early modern era ==
{{main|Early modern era}}

=== Americas ===
{{Main|European colonization of the Americas}}
Beginning with the 1492 arrival of [[Christopher Columbus]] in the Caribbean and gaining control over more territory for over three centuries, the [[Spanish colonization of the Americas|Spanish Empire would expand]] across the [[Caribbean Islands]], half of South America, most of [[Central America]] and much of [[North America]]. The major empires of the American continents were defeated by much smaller Spanish forces. The [[Aztec Empire]] under [[Moctezuma II]] had 200,000 troops under its command, but was defeated by little over 600 [[conquistador]]s.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=429}} The [[Inca Empire]] under [[Atahualpa]] with 60,000 soldiers was defeated by 168 Spaniards, meanwhile.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=429}}

Following an earlier expedition to [[Yucatán Peninsula|Yucatán]] led by [[Juan de Grijalva]] in 1518, Spanish conquistador [[Hernán Cortés]] led an expedition (''entrada'') to Mexico. Two years later, in 1519, Cortés and his retinue set sail for Mexico.<ref name="spanishwars.net">{{Cite web|title=Conquest of the Aztec Empire Part I|url=http://www.spanishwars.net/16th-century-conquest-of-the-aztec-empire-partI.html|access-date=2017-05-06|website=www.spanishwars.net|language=en}}</ref> Cortés made alliances with tributary city-states (''[[altepetl]]'') of the [[Aztec Empire]] as well as their political rivals, particularly the [[Tlaxcaltec]]s and [[Tetzcoco (altepetl)|Tetzcocans]], a former partner in the Aztec Triple Alliance. Other city-states also joined, including [[Cempoala]] and [[Huejotzingo]] and polities bordering [[Lake Texcoco]], the inland lake system of the [[Valley of Mexico]]. The [[Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire|Spanish campaign against the Aztec Empire]] had its final victory on 13 August 1521, when a coalition army of Spanish forces and native [[Tlaxcala (Nahua state)|Tlaxcalan]] warriors led by Cortés and [[Xicotencatl II|Xicotencatl the Younger]] captured the emperor [[Cuauhtémoc]] and [[Tenochtitlan]], the capital of the [[Aztec Triple Alliance|Aztec Empire]]. The [[fall of Tenochtitlan]] marks the beginning of [[New Spain|Spanish rule in central Mexico]], and they established their capital of [[Mexico City]] on the ruins of Tenochtitlan.

After years of preliminary exploration and military skirmishes, 168 [[Spaniards|Spanish]] soldiers under [[conquistador]] [[Francisco Pizarro]], his brothers, and their [[Indigenous peoples of South America|indigenous]] [[Indian auxiliaries|allies]] captured the [[Sapa Inca]] [[Atahualpa]] in the 1532 [[Battle of Cajamarca]]. It was the first step in a long campaign that took decades of fighting but ended in Spanish victory in 1572 and colonization of the region as the [[Viceroyalty of Peru]].

The [[Spanish conquest of the Muisca]] took place from 1537 to 1540.  Meanwhile, the [[Calchaquí Wars]] were a series of military conflicts between the [[Diaguita|Diaguita Confederation]] and the [[Spanish Empire]] in the 1560–1667 period. After many [[Conquest of Chile|initial Spanish successes]] in the [[Arauco War]] against the [[Mapuche]], the [[Battle of Curalaba]] in 1598 and the following [[destruction of the Seven Cities]] marked a turning point in the war leading to the establishment of [[La Frontera, Chile|a clear frontier]] between the Spanish domains and the land of the independent Mapuche.

=== Asia ===

==== Gunpowder empires ====
{{Main|Gunpowder empires}}
[[File:Islamic Gunpowder Empires.jpg|thumb|The gunpowder empires]]
The [[gunpowder empires]] were the [[Ottoman Empire|Ottoman]], [[Safavid Iran|Safavid]], and [[Mughal Empire|Mughal]] empires as they flourished from the 16th century to the 18th century. These three empires were among the strongest and most stable economies of the [[early modern period]], leading to commercial expansion, and greater patronage of culture, while their political and legal institutions were consolidated with an increasing degree of centralisation. The empires underwent a significant increase in per capita income and population, and a sustained pace of technological innovation. They stretched from [[Central Europe]] and [[North Africa]] in the west to between today's modern [[Bangladesh]] and [[Myanmar]] in the east.

Under Sultan [[Selim I]] (1512–1520), the Ottomans defeated the Safavids in the [[Battle of Chaldiran]] (1514).<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=439}} His successor, [[Suleiman the Magnificent]] (1520–1566), the Ottoman Empire marked the peak of its power and prosperity as well as the highest development of its government, social, and economic systems.<ref>{{cite web|date=6 May 2008|title=Ottoman Empire|url=http://www.oxfordislamicstudies.com/article/opr/t125/e1801?_hi=41&_pos=3|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120525072847/http://www.oxfordislamicstudies.com/article/opr/t125/e1801?_hi=41&_pos=3|url-status=dead|archive-date=May 25, 2012|access-date=26 August 2010|publisher=Oxford Islamic Studies Online}}</ref> Already controlling the Balkans, it was able to invade [[Hungary]] and win in the [[Battle of Mohács]] (1526).<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=439}} However, further advancement failed after the [[Siege of Vienna (1529)]].<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=440}} Following naval victories in the [[Battle of Preveza]] (1538) and the [[Battle of Djerba]] (1560), the Ottomans also emerged as the dominant maritime power in the Mediterranean.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=441}} A sailing voyage even reached the [[Aceh Sultanate]] in 1565.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=442}} At the beginning of the 17th century, the empire contained [[Provinces of the Ottoman Empire|32 provinces]] and numerous [[Vassal and tributary states of the Ottoman Empire|vassal states]]. Some of these were later absorbed into the Ottoman Empire, while others were granted various types of autonomy over the course of centuries.<ref group="note">The empire also temporarily gained authority over distant overseas lands through declarations of allegiance to the [[Ottoman dynasty|Ottoman Sultan and Caliph]], such as the [[Ottoman expedition to Aceh|declaration by the Sultan of Aceh]] in 1565, or through temporary acquisitions of islands such as [[Lanzarote]] in the Atlantic Ocean in 1585, [https://web.archive.org/web/20100329023319/http://www.dzkk.tsk.tr/turkce/tarihimiras/AtlantikteTurkDenizciligi.php Turkish Navy Official Website: "Atlantik'te Türk Denizciliği"]</ref>

However, the Ottomans began to face many challenges. The failure to conquer the Safavid Empire forced it to keep forces in the east, while the expansion of the [[Russian Empire]] put pressure on the [[Black Sea]] territories.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=442}} Meanwhile, Western powers began to overtake their maritime capabilities, with the [[Battle of Lepanto]] (1571) being a turning point.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=442}} In 1683, the [[Battle of Vienna]] halted an Ottoman invasion again, with the Christian [[Holy League (1684)|Holy League]] driving the Empire back into the Balkans.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=442}} Despite the Venetian [[Morean War|reconquest of Morea]] ([[Peloponnese]]) in the 1680s and [[Ottoman–Venetian War (1714–1718)#Ottoman reconquest of the Morea|it was recovered]] in 1715, while the island of [[Venetian rule in the Ionian Islands|Corfu under Venetian rule]] remained the only Greek island not conquered by the Ottomans. The Ottoman Empire still remained the largest power in the Mediterranean and the Middle East.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=443}}

The Safavid dynasty ruled [[Iran|Persia]] from 1501 to 1722 (experiencing a brief restoration from 1729 to 1736). It ruled from the [[Black Sea]] to the [[Hindu Kush]], with more than 50 million inhabitants.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=443}} Originating from [[Caucasus|Caucasian]] warriors called the [[Qizilbash]], they conquered [[Armenia]] in 1501, most of Persia by 1504, parts of [[Uzbekistan]] in 1511, and unsuccessfully fighting over Caucasus and [[Mesopotamia]] until 1555.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=444}} However, [[Baghdad]] was recaptured in 1623.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=444}} The expansion of Russia in the north eventually started to pose a threat.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=446}} The Empire was finally defeated by and divided between the Ottomans and the Russians in 1722–23.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=447}}

The [[Mughal Empire]] was an empire in [[South Asia]].<ref name="Richards1995-2">{{Citation|last=Richards|first=John F.|title=The Mughal Empire|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HHyVh29gy4QC&pg=PA2|page=2|year=1995|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-56603-2}} Quote: "Although the first two Timurid emperors and many of their noblemen were recent migrants to the subcontinent, the dynasty and the empire itself became indisputably Indian. The interests and futures of all concerned were in India, not in ancestral homelands in the Middle East or Central Asia. Furthermore, the Mughal empire emerged from the Indian historical experience. It was the end product of a millennium of Muslim conquest, colonization, and state-building in the Indian subcontinent."</ref> For some two centuries, the empire stretched from the outer fringes of the Indus basin in the west, northern [[Afghanistan]] in the northwest, and [[Kashmir]] in the north, to the [[highland]]s of present-day [[Assam]] and [[Bangladesh]] in the east, and the uplands of the [[Deccan Plateau]] in [[South India]].<ref name="Stein2010-12">{{Citation|last=Stein|first=Burton|title=A History of India|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QY4zdTDwMAQC&pg=PA159|pages=159–|year=2010|publisher=John Wiley & Sons|isbn=978-1-4443-2351-1|author-link=Burton Stein}} Quote: "The realm so defined and governed was a vast territory of some {{convert|750000|sqmi|km2|disp=sqbr}}, ranging from the frontier with Central Asia in northern Afghanistan to the northern uplands of the Deccan plateau, and from the Indus basin on the west to the Assamese highlands in the east."</ref> In 1505, Central Asian invaders had entered the [[Indo-Gangetic Plain]] and established the Empire under [[Akbar]] (1556–1605).<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=447}} The neglect of northern defences allowed the Persians under [[Nader Shah]] to invade in 1739, with the capital [[Delhi]] sacked.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=450–451}}

=== East Asia ===
Under the [[Ming dynasty]] (1368–1644), China's population and economy grew.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=451}} While the [[Portuguese Empire]] was at first successfully kept out, [[Wokou|Japanese pirates]] began to attack the coast, forcing co-operation with the Portuguese who established a trading settlement at [[Macau]] in 1554.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=452}} Northern [[Mongols|Mongol]] and [[Jurchen people]] established a coalition to invade the country, reaching [[Beijing]] in 1550.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=452}} In 1592, the [[Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)|Japanese invaded Korea]], while rebellions emerged in China.<ref name="Holslag 2018"/>{{rp|p=453}}

=== Europe ===
[[File:LocationSilesia.png|thumb|[[Silesia]]'s position in Europe (in red)]]
In 1700, [[Charles II of Spain]] died, naming [[Phillip of Anjou]], [[Louis XIV]]'s grandson, his heir. Charles' decision was not well met by the British, who believed that Louis would use the opportunity to ally [[France]] and [[Spain]] and attempt to take over Europe. Britain formed the [[Grand Alliance (League of Augsburg)|Grand Alliance]] with [[Holland]], [[Austria]] and a majority of the [[List of states in the Holy Roman Empire|German states]] and declared war against Spain in 1702. The [[War of the Spanish Succession]] lasted 11 years, and ended when the [[Treaty of Utrecht]] was signed in 1714.<ref>{{cite book|last=Frey|first=Marsha|author2=Linda Frey|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|year=1995|edition=illustrated|isbn=0-313-27884-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wrJmCvFY6ocC|access-date=2009-01-29|title=The Treaties of the War of the Spanish Succession}}</ref>

Less than 50 years later, in 1740, war broke out again, sparked by the invasion of [[Silesia]], part of [[Archduchy of Austria|Austria]], by King [[Frederick the Great]] of [[Kingdom of Prussia|Prussia]]. The [[British Empire]], the [[Dutch Republic]], and the [[Kingdom of Hungary (1526–1867)|Kingdom of Hungary]] supported [[Maria Theresa]]. Over the next eight years, these and other states participated in the [[War of the Austrian Succession]], until a treaty was signed, allowing Prussia to keep Silesia.<ref>{{cite book|last=Dupuy|first=Richard Ernes|author2=Trevor Nevitt Dupuy|title=The Encyclopedia of Military History: From 3500 B.C. to the Present|publisher=Harper & Row|year=1970|page=630|isbn=9780060111397|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WYpAAAAAIAAJ|access-date=2009-01-30}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last=Rosner|first=Lisa|author2=John Theibault|title=A Short History of Europe, 1600-1815: Search for a Reasonable World|publisher=M.E. Sharpe|year=2000|edition=illustrated|isbn=0-7656-0328-4|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KVbsFMmLAyAC|access-date=2009-01-30|page=292}}</ref> The [[Seven Years' War]] began when Theresa dissolved her alliance with Britain and allied with [[France]] and [[Russia]]. In 1763, Britain won the war, claiming [[Canada]] and land east of the [[Mississippi]]. Prussia also kept Silesia.<ref>{{cite book|last=Marston|first=Daniel|title=The Seven Years' War|publisher=Osprey Publishing|year=2001|edition=illustrated|isbn=1-84176-191-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3KwvINgeg_sC|access-date=2008-01-30}}</ref>

=== Oceania ===
{{Main|History of Oceania}}
[[File:Van Diemen's Land 1852.jpg|thumb|left|[[Van Diemen's Land]] in 1852]]
Interest in the geography of the [[Southern Hemisphere]] began to increase in the 18th century.<ref>{{cite book |last=Daniels |first=Patrica S |author2=Stephen G Hyslop |author3=Douglas Brinkley |author4=Esther Ferington |author5=Lee Hassig |author6=Dale-Marie Herring |editor=Toni Eugene |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=d5gPAQAACAAJ |title=Almanac of World History |year=2003 |publisher=National Geographic Society |isbn=0-7922-5092-3 |page=214 }}{{Dead link|date=May 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> In 1642, [[Dutch people|Dutch]] [[navigator]] [[Abel Tasman]] was commissioned to explore the [[Southern Hemisphere]]; during his voyages, Tasman discovered the island of [[Van Diemen's Land]], which was later named [[Tasmania]], the [[Australia]]n coast, and [[New Zealand]] in 1644.<ref>{{cite book|last=Porter|first=Malcolm|author2=Keith Lye|title=Australia and the Pacific|publisher=Cherrytree Books|year=2007|edition=illustrated and revised 2007|page=20|isbn=978-1-84234-460-6|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=edd2vPFKsUMC|access-date=2008-01-30}}</ref> Captain [[James Cook]] was commissioned in 1768 to observe a [[solar eclipse]] in [[Tahiti]] and sailed into [[Stingray Harbor]] on [[Eastern states of Australia|Australia's east coast]] in 1770, claiming the land for the [[British Crown]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Kitson|first=Arthur|title=The Life of Captain James Cook|publisher=Kessinger Publishing|year=2004|pages=84–5|isbn=1-4191-6947-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NNh-YFw_bZ0C|access-date=2008-01-30}}</ref> Settlements in [[Australia]] began in 1788 when Britain began to utilize the country for the deportation of [[convicts]],<ref>{{cite book|last=King|first=Jonathan|title=The First Settlement: The Convict Village that Founded Australia 1788-90|publisher=Macmillan|year=1984|isbn=0-333-38080-0|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FhOCAAAACAAJ}}</ref> with the first free settles arriving in 1793.<ref>{{cite book|last=Currer-Briggs|first=Noel|title=Worldwide Family History|publisher=Routledge|year=1982|edition=illustrated|page=180|isbn=0-7100-0934-8|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=mJg9AAAAIAAJ}}</ref> Likewise New Zealand became a home for hunters seeking [[whale]]s and [[Pinniped|seal]]s in the 1790s with later [[non-commercial]] [[Human settlement|settlements]] by the [[Scottish people|Scottish]] in the 1820s and 1830s.<ref>{{cite book |last=Daniels |first=Patrica S |author2=Stephen G Hyslop |author3=Douglas Brinkley |author4=Esther Ferington |author5=Lee Hassig |author6=Dale-Marie Herring |editor=Toni Eugene |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=d5gPAQAACAAJ |title=Almanac of World History |year=2003 |publisher=National Geographic Society |isbn=0-7922-5092-3 |page=216 }}{{Dead link|date=May 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>

== Modern era ==
{{main|Modern era}}

=== Revolutionary waves ===
{{Main|Age of Revolution||}}

The [[Atlantic Revolutions]] were a [[revolutionary wave]] in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries. It took place in both the Americas and Europe. Following the [[Age of Enlightenment]], ideas critical of [[Absolutism (European history)|absolutist monarchies]] began to spread, spreading [[liberalism]]. The first of these was the [[Corsican Republic|Corsican Revolution]] (1755–1769), which led to the [[Corsican Constitution|first modern constitution]] and lead to [[Women's suffrage|female suffrage]], inspired by the thought of [[Jean-Jacques Rousseau]]. In the [[American Revolution]] (1765–1783), American [[Thirteen Colonies|colonies]] of the [[British Empire]] rose against [[No taxation without representation|taxation without representation]] and declared that [[all men are created equal]]. Other revolutions included the [[Geneva Revolution of 1782]], [[Dutch Patriot Revolt, 1787|Revolt of Dutch Patriots]] (1785), [[Liège Revolution]] (1789&ndash;1795), [[Brabant Revolution]] (1790), [[Haitian Revolution]] (1791–1804), [[Batavian Revolution]] (1795), [[Curaçao Slave Revolt of 1795|Slave revolt in Curaçao]] (1795), [[Fédon's rebellion]] (1796), [[Society of the United Scotsmen|Scottish Rebellion]] (1797), [[Irish Rebellion of 1798|Irish Rebellion]] (1798), [[French invasion of Switzerland|Helvetic Revolution]] (1798), and [[Altamuran Revolution]] (1799), [[1811 German Coast uprising]] (1811), and the [[Norwegian War of Independence]] (1814). There were smaller upheavals in Switzerland, Russia, and Brazil. The revolutionaries in each country knew of the others and to some degree were inspired by or emulated them.<ref>Laurent Dubois and Richard Rabinowitz, eds. ''Revolution!: The Atlantic World Reborn'' (2011)</ref>

The [[French Revolutionary Wars]] were a series of sweeping military conflicts lasting from 1792 until 1802 and resulting from the [[French Revolution]]. They pitted [[French First Republic|France]] against Great Britain, the [[Holy Roman Empire]], [[Kingdom of Prussia|Prussia]], [[Russian Empire|Russia]], and several other monarchies. They are divided in two periods: the [[War of the First Coalition]] (1792–1797) and the [[War of the Second Coalition]] (1798–1802). Initially confined to Europe, the fighting gradually assumed a global dimension. After a decade of constant warfare and aggressive diplomacy, France had conquered territories in the  [[Italian Peninsula]], the [[Low Countries]] and the Rhineland in Europe and was retroceded [[Louisiana (New France)|Louisiana]] in North America. French success in these conflicts ensured the spread of revolutionary principles over much of Europe.

[[File:EmpireFrench.png|thumb|The French colonial empire was the second largest empire in the world behind the British Empire.]]

The [[Coup of 18 Brumaire]] brought General [[Napoleon|Napoleon Bonaparte]] to power as [[First Consul]] of [[France]] and in the view of most [[historian]]s ended the [[French Revolution]]. The [[Napoleonic Wars]] (1803–1815) were a series of major conflicts pitting the [[First French Empire|French Empire]] and its allies, led by Napoleon I, against a fluctuating array of [[Coalition forces of the Napoleonic Wars|European powers formed into various coalitions]]. It produced a brief period of French domination over most of continental Europe. The wars stemmed from the unresolved disputes associated with the [[French Revolution]] and its [[French Revolutionary Wars|resultant conflict]]. The wars are often categorised into five conflicts, each termed after the coalition that fought Napoleon: the [[War of the Third Coalition|Third Coalition]] (1805), the [[War of the Fourth Coalition|Fourth]] (1806–07), the [[War of the Fifth Coalition|Fifth]] (1809), the [[War of the Sixth Coalition|Sixth]] (1813–14), and the [[Hundred Days|Seventh]] (1815).

The [[Peninsular War]] with France, which resulted from the [[Kingdom of Spain under Joseph Bonaparte|Napoleonic occupation of Spain]], caused [[Criollo people|Spanish Creoles]] in [[Spanish America]] to question their allegiance to Spain, stoking independence movements that culminated in various [[Spanish American wars of independence]] (1808–1833), which were primarily fought between opposing groups of colonists and only secondarily against Spanish forces. At the same time, the Portuguese monarchy relocated to [[Colonial Brazil|Brazil]] during Portugal's French occupation. After the royal court returned to [[Lisbon]], the [[prince regent]], [[Pedro I of Brazil|Pedro]], remained in Brazil and in 1822 successfully declared himself emperor of a newly independent [[Brazilian Empire]].

[[Revolutions during the 1820s]] included the [[Carbonari]] in [[Italy]], the [[Trienio Liberal]] in [[Spain]], the [[Liberal Revolution of 1820]] in the [[Kingdom of Portugal]], the [[Greek War of Independence]], and the [[Decembrist revolt]] in the [[Russian Empire]]. Followed by these, the [[Revolutions of 1830]] were an included the [[Belgian Revolution]] in the [[United Kingdom of the Netherlands]], the [[July Revolution]] in [[Kingdom of France|France]], the [[November Uprising]] in the [[Congress Poland]], and the [[Ustertag]] in [[Switzerland]]. The [[Revolutions of 1848]] in turn were the most widespread [[revolutionary wave]] in [[European history]]. They included the [[March Revolution (Denmark)|March Revolution]], [[French Revolution of 1848|French Revolution]], [[German revolutions of 1848–1849|German revolutions]], the [[Revolutions of 1848 in the Italian states|Revolutions in the Italian states]], [[Greater Poland uprising (1848)|Greater Poland uprising]], [[March Unrest]], [[Revolutions of 1848 in the Austrian Empire|Revolutions in the Austrian Empire]], [[Praieira revolt]], [[Revolution of 1848 in Luxembourg|Revolution in Luxembourg]], [[Moldavian Revolution of 1848|Moldavian Revolution]], [[Wallachian Revolution of 1848|Wallachian Revolution]], [[Chartism]], and the [[Young Ireland rebellion]].

=== Great power competition ===
Inspired by the rebellions in the 1820s and 1830s against the outcome of the [[Congress of Vienna]], the [[Italian unification]] process was precipitated by the revolutions of 1848.
